issue_title: Optional pants modules need a way to register pantsbuild source roots for targets they export
issue_body: Right now source roots are registered globally (via contrib/BUILD instead of via plugin - which is a secondary issue).  Since they are registered globally, any invocation of pants using the checked-in pants.ini will need to have all contrib plugins loaded that define source roots for the targets they export.  This conflicts with the goals of - for example - the self-publish CI check which tries to verify all plugins are publishable and consumable independently of one another.  Devise and deploy a means to achieve both simple contrib addition and separation for the purposes of certain tests.

issue_title: Daemon invalidation bug for create directories
issue_body:
A sequence of events like the following:

1. create new directory under an existing directory (not at the root)
2. create BUILD file in that new directory
3. add a dependency on the BUILD file to some other existing BUILD file

seems to (sometimes?) result in a failure to locate targets in the new BUILD file (most likely because the parent directory of the new directory was not properly invalidated).

I've attached a log from a session that exhibited the problem. Running a command involving the target edited in the equivalent of step 3 (from above) results in an error indicating that the BUILD file from step 2 is not found.
 
[Failure log: pantsd.log.txt](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/files/1062789/pantsd.log.txt)

```

Probably involves some kind of try-catch in jline, and pulling the fix through to sbt and then our zinc wrapper.

issue_title: Port KA Lite start/stop+daemon logic
issue_body:
## Summary

We have some pretty sensible and tested logic in KA Lite, which we can port.

Possibly adding a `--force` flag for clearing out startup locks or a `--force-clear-locks`. Making a confirmation dialogue CLI, which should ensure that no one abuses this flag in a script for startups. We need to get to the bottom of brokenness with startup locks.

Also needs docs that state various scenarios in case we can think of something that theoretically makes the server unstartable.

Needs doing ASAP.

We can possibly use https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/pull/746 for this

## System information

 - Version: 0.5.x

issue_title: Validating JSON blob fields in REST API
issue_body:
I noticed that the `Exam` has a JSON field for `question_sources`, and has a doc string explaining what the shape of `question_sources` should be:

https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/blob/3b4c56f3409f1f1133eb73ebdad72729749a37b9/kolibri/core/exams/models.py#L31-L39

But the `ExamSerializer` does not seem to validate `question_sources` when POSTing a new Exam.

Maybe we should validate `question_sources` and other un-typed JSON fields in the API.

What are some strategies for doing this?

issue_title: Authors should be able to lock to a specific version of a layer when building
issue_body:
Idea for layers is to be able to "freeze" to a specific version, similar to what a pip freeze would do.

```yaml
includes: ['layer:basic@<REV>']
```

Where `<REV>` is either a git sha1sum, tag, or branch

issue_title: Consider generating code from Swagger API spec
issue_body:
I recently tried generating a [JS client](https://www.npmjs.com/package/docker-client) for the Docker API from the [Swagger spec](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/moby/moby/master/api/swagger.yaml) in the main [moby repository](https://github.com/moby/moby/) repository. Having used it extensively for some integration testing work, I found that the Swagger spec is pretty mature, and that a client autogenerated from it is quite usable for simple as well as complex tasks.

Perhaps it would be a good idea to substitute the "low-level API" part of this project with something automatically generated from the Swagger spec? This would be beneficial in several ways:

 - A large community of users relying on code generated from the Swagger spec would help to feed back into improving the accuracy of the spec (and hence all other generated clients + the API docs)
 - Less duplicated effort in maintaining documentation and signatures for endpoints
 - If integration tests are specified as a sequence of inputs/expected outputs against the spec endpoints, multiple client implementations can share a test suite

There is a Python code generator in the [swagger-codegen](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-codegen/) project that can be used as a starting point. The generator currently chokes on the spec; I need to debug whether the issue is in the spec or the code generator (I had to do a similar exercise when generating the JS client).

issue_title: Reducing a joint gaussian distribution created from a LinearGaussianBayesianNetwork shows unstable behaviour.
issue_body:
Reducing a joint gaussian distribution created from a linear gaussian bayesian network shows different outcomes when running the same program multiple times This [example](http://termbin.com/vzos) shows this behaviour. 
I suspect that either the joint gaussian distribution is not created the same in all runs, or there is a small error in the reduce function. I checked the math with a book I have, and I think the reduce function is working properly. 
It is harder to check if the joint distribution is created properly, because it changes the order of variables in different runs. This gives different orders of rows and columns in the covariance matrix. (Which should not be a problem)

issue_title: Order of instances in generated designspace doc
issue_body:
I am finding that some applications, when loading a variation font, are loading the first named instance in the font, which may or may not be the best instance to represent the font. 

This is based on the designspace document that is generated from the original Glyphs Source. However, as far as I can tell, there's no relationship between the instance order in Glyphs and the instance order in the designspace document. 

Would it be possible to set the order of the instances in the designspace document based on the Glyphs instance order?

issue_title: Support "max_pages" and "max_items" on "pages" generator
issue_body:
Version 0.5 will require explicit call to the ```pages``` method on the executor. This method should accept "max_pages" and "max_items" kwargs and behave lake the example: 
```
for item in resp().pages(max_pages=2, max_items=40):
    print(item)
# in this example, the for loop will stop after two pages are fetched or 40 items are yielded, 
# witch ever comes first.
```

issue_title: nifgen.Session.define_user_standard_waveform() has no numpy version
issue_body:
nifgen.Session.define_user_standard_waveform() takes in a list of floats which needs to be copied element by element to a ctypes buffer before being passed to the driver. This is slow.

Python bindings could have logic to accept a numpy.ndarray instead and avoid the element-by-element copy.

issue_title: Add support for C structs
Data not converted to bytes when starting proxy
issue_body:
When starting a proxy with python3, an exception is thrown saying that the data must not be unicode:

```
2017-09-12T14:19:58+0200 Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/twisted/python/log.py", line 103, in callWithLogger
    return callWithContext({"system": lp}, func, *args, **kw)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/twisted/python/log.py", line 86, in callWithContext
    return context.call({ILogContext: newCtx}, func, *args, **kw)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/twisted/python/context.py", line 122, in callWithContext
    return self.currentContext().callWithContext(ctx, func, *args, **kw)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/twisted/python/context.py", line 85, in callWithContext
    return func(*args,**kw)
--- <exception caught here> ---
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/twisted/internet/asyncioreactor.py", line 136, in _readOrWrite
    why = method()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/twisted/internet/tcp.py", line 586, in doConnect
    self._connectDone()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/twisted/internet/tcp.py", line 615, in _connectDone
    self.protocol.makeConnection(self)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/twisted/internet/protocol.py", line 494, in makeConnection
    self.connectionMade()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/autobahn/twisted/websocket.py", line 94, in connectionMade
    self._connectionMade()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/autobahn/websocket/protocol.py", line 3328, in _connectionMade
    self.startProxyConnect()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/autobahn/websocket/protocol.py", line 3354, in startProxyConnect
    self.sendData(request)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/autobahn/websocket/protocol.py", line 1297, in sendData
    self.transport.write(data)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/twisted/internet/_newtls.py", line 191, in write
    FileDescriptor.write(self, bytes)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/twisted/internet/abstract.py", line 348, in write
    raise TypeError("Data must not be unicode")
builtins.TypeError: Data must not be unicode
```

The origin of this error lies in module autobahn.wamp.protocol in function startProxy (line 3359):
```python
    def startProxyConnect(self):
        """
        Connect to explicit proxy.
        """
        # construct proxy connect HTTP request
        #
        request = "CONNECT %s:%d HTTP/1.1\x0d\x0a" % (self.factory.host.encode("utf-8"), self.factory.port)
        request += "Host: %s:%d\x0d\x0a" % (self.factory.host.encode("utf-8"), self.factory.port)
        request += "\x0d\x0a"

        self.log.debug("{request}", request=request)

        self.sendData(request)
```

Where the request parameter must be of type bytes. The simple solution is to prefix the strings with a 'b':
```python
    def startProxyConnect(self):
        """
        Connect to explicit proxy.
        """
        # construct proxy connect HTTP request
        #
        request = b"CONNECT %s:%d HTTP/1.1\x0d\x0a" % (self.factory.host.encode("utf-8"), self.factory.port)
        request += b"Host: %s:%d\x0d\x0a" % (self.factory.host.encode("utf-8"), self.factory.port)
        request += b"\x0d\x0a"

        self.log.debug("{request}", request=request)

        self.sendData(request)
```

This solves the issue entirely and does not break backwards compatibility (tested it locally).
I can create a pull request if you want, although it's very easy to fix.

Cheers
- Brecht

issue_title: Test CleanerVersion migration support for Django =< 1.6 (e.g. with South)
issue_body:
Django 1.7 introduced migrations. CleanerVersion has been adapted to support these (in PR #24). 
However, has not been tested whether CleanerVersion on Django 1.6 supports migrations with South.
If it does not, the according fixes should be applied if possible.

Initial note by @jczulian:
"Since Django migration is inherited from South (migration system used before 1.7) I wonder what is happening when using Django 1.6.X together with South? Does South do something similar to Django 1.7? Therefore should we test alongside the Django's version number if South is present and act in the same way as we do when running under Django 1.7"

issue_title: request: Custom 502 error page on per-application basis
issue_body:
I would like to replace the current default 502 Bad gateway page with a custom page on a per application basis.

*The use case is as follows:*
- Whenever we do a `git push deis` and update the application, in the transistion period between two versions where the old version is shutted down and the new version is started up, the page is replaced with a "502 Bad Gateway" nginx page. Instead of this page it would be useful to replace it with a custom HTML page (similar to what, for instance, Twitter and Reddit has) until the server is up and running again.

Ideally, it shouldn't be needed, but until #1829 rolling version update is added, I think this is a good solution.

I'm thinking it should be possible to upload a single html file. If the html file needs external resources, then that should either be stored on an external CDN or embedded in the page (base64).

issue_title: Fix test in test_vmc_handler.py
issue_body:
### What is wrong?
The expected `TransactionFailed` error in here in `test_vmc_handler.py` is not caused by expected case.
```python
# if a header is added before its parent header is added, `add_header` should fail
    # TransactionFailed raised when assertions fail
    with pytest.raises(TransactionFailed):
        header_parent_not_added = mk_testing_colhdr(
            vmc,
            shard_id,
            header0_1.hash,
            1,
        )
        add_header_constant_call(vmc, header_parent_not_added)
```

After some debugging, it turns out that this error is caused by wrong period number provided, instead of the expected cause(`if a header is added before its parent header is added, `add_header` should fail`).
Cause: Since a local function call(i.e., no real transaction broadcasted) in web3.py will use the latest mined block number as `block.number`, this cause the `addHeader` to fetch the wrong block hash with the lates mined block number(should be latest mined block number+1) and results in assertion failure in contract.

### How can it be fixed

Check if we can customize environment parameters(block number) in web3.py or come up with different cases that would have the expected result(`if a header is added before its parent header is added, `add_header` should fail`).

issue_title: Why use two different build scripts: build.sh and bld.bat?
issue_body: I've been trying to port a few of my projects to be built and distributed with conda and binstar, and I am confused as to why [conda supports](http://conda.pydata.org/docs/build.html) two different build scripts: `build.sh` and `bld.bat`. I understand that `build.sh` is intended to be executed on posix systems and `bld.bat` on windows, but why not simply have a single python build script (e.g. `build.py`)? If conda is being  used to build a package, python is available. Additionally, a single `build.py` seems like it would simplify things for the package maintainer -- they would only have to maintain a single build script.

issue_title: [ENH] make activate work if old env doesn't exist any more
issue_body:
I have spent a long time convincing myself that I am a rare, if not unique, case in the way I keep getting myself into this mixed up situation of having an "activated" conda environment that I've deleted.  But I've done it so many times in the past week that I'm thinking other people must do it at least occasionally.  This is what it looks like:

```bash
(envs/default) $ rm -Rf envs/default                  # delete a bad env, but OOPS, it is still activated

(envs/default) $ anaconda-project run                 # dang, that's not going to work.
-bash: anaconda-project: command not found

(envs/default) $ source activate root                 # try to switch to root, but activate is not on the path
-bash: activate: No such file or directory

(envs/default) $ source ~/anaconda/bin/activate root  # well, I know where it lives, so I'll do that

CondaValueError: Value error: Could not find environment: /Users/ijstokes/teaching/python/pycon2017-anaconda-project-data-science-apps/mnist_digit_recognition/envs/default

-bash: sed: command not found
-bash: grep: command not found
-bash: grep: command not found
-bash: grep: command not found
-bash: sed: command not found
```

Oh dear, now my shell is pooched.  Close and re-open.

So maybe if conda were in its own env that was always on the path, and the activate script came from that location instead of from the "active" env, then this problem would go away.

But if that is a long way off, or if the isolated-conda-env idea doesn't solve this problem, then perhaps doing `source /path/to/another/env/bin/activate foo` could either be made to work as expected or to fail gently instead of catastrophically.

issue_title: Installation on Python 3 misses Cython dependency (0.9.0 regression)
issue_body:
Since release 0.9.0, `pip install pysam` on Python 3 fails because Cython is missing:

```
$ virtualenv -p `which python3.5` py35
$ . py35/bin/activate
$ pip install pysam
Collecting pysam
  Using cached pysam-0.9.0.tar.gz
    Complete output from command python setup.py egg_info:
    checking for gcc... gcc
    checking whether the C compiler works... yes
...
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
      File "/tmp/pip-build-8096gdno/pysam/setup.py", line 290, in <module>
        .format(fn))
    ValueError: no cython installed, but can not find pysam/chtslib.c.Make sure that cython is installed when building from the repository
    
    ----------------------------------------
Command "python setup.py egg_info" failed with error code 1 in /tmp/pip-build-8096gdno/pysam
```

Release 0.8.4 was good.

I tested Python 3.3, 3.4, and 3.5, they all fail in the same way. Python 2.7 seems to be good though, I'm not sure why.

It looks like the `requires` argument to setup is ignored (it lists `cython (>=0.21)`). I have no idea what causes this, but there have been quite some changes to `setup.py` since 0.8.4.

issue_title: Support for Synopsys VCS
issue_body:
I have taken a quick look into supporting the Synopsys VCS simulator.
It looks like similar to Cadence's ``cds.lib``, VCS needs a file ``.synopsys_vss.setup`` for the library name-to-path mapping. http://salinasv.blogspot.de/2011/05/simulating-mixed-language-hdl-using-vcs.html has some info about it.
It would be nice if there was a class for reading and writing such a file, like the ``CDSFile`` class in ``cds_file.py`` for Incisive.

issue_title: [App Service] Linux web apps don't appear to be configured correctly when using local git
issue_body: Using the CLI, I created a new Linux App Service plan, and a web app that is configured to use local git for deployments. After doing a Git push and setting the start file (which hopefully won't be necessary soon), I launched the site and got the following error: After not being able to figure out what the issue was, I opened up the portal and noticed that there wasn't a runtime stack selected on the `Docker Container` tab (see image below). I explicitly set it to `Node.js 6.6.0`, at which point, everything worked great. That makes me wonder whether the `az appservice web create` command configures the app correctly when relying on a default runtime stack. I've not had any issues when deploying a custom Docker container, so maybe the `az appservice web config container update` command doesn't the necessary work to configure the app correctly? Oddly enough, when I ran the `az appservice web config appsettings show` command, I noticed that following output, so it seemed like it detected the use of Node after the Git push, but it didn't setup the runtime stack container right? ``` WEBSITE_NODE_DEFAULT_VERSION ------------------------------ 6.9.1 ```

issue_title: Documentation for vm extension set lacks important details
issue_body:
az vm extension set supports:

issue_title: Ephemeral volumes aren't raided in all cases
issue_body:
If you use an instance type with two ephemeral drives, cgcloud will install mdadm during `create -IT` and then take the image. So mdadm is present in the image and will not need to be installed again when running `recreate` from the image. But if you use an instance type with 0 or 1 ephemeral drives for `create -IT`, mdadm will not be needed at that time and so must be installed during `recreate`. If, between `create` and `recreate` an update to the mdadm package is published, apt will fail to install mdadm during `recreate` because the on-disk package index is stale and refers to an non-existant .deb.

The solution is to install mdadm unconditionally during the regular setup. There still is a chance that the early installation of mdadm at boot time will fail during recreate if the package index on the base image is stale, too.

issue_title: version.py on master is wrong
issue_body:
```diff
--- version.py	(revision d83191da508a9ebad66f497c76471a6892440bdf)
+++ version.py	(revision )
@@ -12,4 +12,4 @@
 # See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
 # limitations under the License.
 
-version = '1.0.3a1'
+version = '1.1.0a1'
```

After a release is branched off, master's minor version (the second number in a version string) should be incremented. On a release branch, only the hotfix (the 3rd number) is incremented after a releases.

```

1--2--3 master branch
    \
     4--5 releases/1.0.x branch
     ^
     |
     +--- releases/1.0.0 tag
```

Commits 1 and 2 are general commits, most likely merge commits that integrate features or bug fixes into the master branch before the release. They will have `version=1.0.0a1` in version.py or setup.py.

Commit 3 changes version.py from `1.0.0a1` to `1.1.0a1`.

Commit 4 changes version.py from `1.0.0a1` to `1.0.0`.

Commit 5 changes version.py `1.0.0` to `1.0.1a1`.

issue_title: Typo in translatable string in oscar.core.validators
Proposal: C++/CX support
issue_body:
Sorry for posting this here, but I found no better alternative, due to the highly cross-platform and cross-language nature of the issue.

**Could .NET Core support C++/CX on all supported platform?**

The sheer awesome feature of C++/CX is the ability to wrap native C/C++ applications/libraries with managed-friendly material. With the recent release of PowerShell Core, this feature could really strengthen the value of PowerShell. Though my background is scientific HPC, so my main motivation is FFTW, BLAS and alike libraries, the audience of C/C++ wrapped with script-friendly material is far larger, than the HPC community. Some of the tools I would enjoy being wrapped as Cmdlets:

- Compilers such as MSVC (cl.exe/link.exe) when refactored as a library, so that the well known command-line executable is only one of the consuming end, would enable a script-friendly Cmdlet front-end as well. Propagating compiler errors (and warnings when opted for) as exceptions would allow for script writers not to rely solely on exit codes for information, neither to resort to regex voodoo to obtain additional information.

- Makefiles generators such as [CMake](https://cmake.org/) could be wrapped with a nice ConvertFrom-CMake cmdlet to convert makefiles into [MSBuild](https://github.com/Microsoft/msbuild) or [Ninja](https://ninja-build.org/) scripts with tab-completion of available generators on the system. Note, that while MSBuild has a PS wrapping project ([psbuild](https://github.com/ligershark/psbuild)), but features such as Write-Progress support (a long time dream of mine) requires programmatic access to the internals of the MSBuild machinery.

These are only a few examples of what I head in mind off the top of my head, but surely, there are a horde of C/C++ tools and utilities out there that could benefit from some managed and script love.

Support "new config file" when no vhost / servername found
issue_body:
Using the Apache configurator on our acme-client test machine, I got a cert for one of my hostnames that didn't already exist in any of the Apache configs. At one point I got a screen that said "We were unable to find a vhost with a ServerName or Address of abcde.crud.net. Which virtual host would you like to choose?"

None of the virtual hosts was really suitable, since I hadn't yet set one up for that hostname on that machine. Ideally we should offer a "new vhost" option that would create a file in the appropriate directory. I realize this is probably quite fraught: How do we know if the new file will be included into the main config; what do we set its documentroot to.

As a stopgap, to make the messaging clearer, maybe we should add language like: "If you haven't yet set up Apache for <host> on this machine, we suggest doing that now and running letsencrypt again."

issue_title: Using dialog in small terminals
issue_body:
We have hard coded that the dialog window be 24 characters tall and 72 characters wide in `letsencrypt/display/util.py`. If the terminal is smaller than that, `dialog` raises an exception. This exception may be unnecessary because the requested `dialog` widget may fit in a terminal smaller than 24 x 72.

`dialog` has the ability to [autosize widgets](http://pythondialog.sourceforge.net/doc/Dialog_class_overview.html#autowidgetsize) which we might want to use. If we take this route, we should do the following:

1. Make sure this works on all system with both new and old version of `dialog`.
2. Make sure we're happy with how the autosized widgets look.

issue_title: Handle multiple "up" links
issue_body:
After issuing a new cert, the client will get a link to the issuer cert using a link with rel="up". We expect that there will be multiple "up" links for the multiple intermediate certs. One intermediate cert will be signed by the ISRG Root. The other intermediate cert will be signed by the IdenTrust Root. In production rollout we'll want the client to choose the IdenTrust-signed intermediate, which will be trusted by real clients.

The client should probably fetch each issuer cert listed in an "up" link, parse it, and prioritize based on that intermediate's Issuer field, comparing it against a baked-in list of roots that it knows to be widely trusted.

issue_title: Support for Message headers
issue_body:
Description
===========
[KIP-82](https://cwiki.apache.org/confluence/display/KAFKA/KIP-82+-+Add+Record+Headers) adds support for message headers. The librdkafka support is in a PR here https://github.com/edenhill/librdkafka/pull/1480

I wanted to track python support here and discuss the python api when it lands upstream.

The headers are an ordered multimap with `string key -> binary value` where a key can be repeated.

There are a couple ways to support this in producer and message api.

List of Tuples/Lists:
```
headers_to_send = [('key1', b'value1'), ('key2', b'value2'), ('key1', b'repeated_key')]
producer.produce('topic_name', b'message_value', headers=headers_to_send)
```

Ordered dict. Python doesn't have a multimap built in, but we could build one or just use an ordered map where values are always nested in lists.
```
headers_to_send = OrderedMultiDict()
headers_to_send['key1'] = [b'value1',  b'repeated_key']
headers_to_send['key2'] = [b'value1']

OR

headers_to_send = OrderedMultiDict()
headers_to_send['key1'] = b'value1'
headers_to_send['key1'].append(b'repeated_key')
headers_to_send['key2'] = b'value1'

producer.produce('topic_name', b'message_value', headers=headers_to_send)
```

What ever the api for producer the consumer `msg.headers()` should return the same format as the producer input. Also for multi-version kafka support mirror the `msg.timestamp()` return flag of `(int, X)` could be used.
```
msg = consumer.poll()
supported_flag, list_of_tuples = msg.headers()
print(list_of_tuples)  # [('key1', b'value1'), ('key2', b'value2'), ('key1', b'repeated_key')]
```

This isn't an exhaustive list of options but i wanted to get the discussion started.

issue_title: Records can't be pickled because of the Metaclass used to create them.
issue_body:
I've got a mostly-working fix for this, but the loaded record doesn't compare equal to the dumped record because a new type with the same name gets created (obviously).  I'd like to propose a change to the equality check for `RecordClass` so that it more closely matches the equality check of `namedtuple`s.  `namedtuple` instances compare equal if corresponding fields (by index, not name), compare equal, essentially as if they were normal tuples.  I'd like to propose an update to the equality check of `RecordClass` to one of:
 - Records are equal iff all required fields compare equal (compared according to index, not name) and all optional fields compare equal, compared according to name (basically dicts mapping optional field name to value compare equal).  This most closely matches `namedtuple`, but does allow you to do weird things.
 - Same as previous, but compare all fields by name, rather than comparing required fields positionally.
 - Same as previous, but also require that the Record type names match.

Thoughts?  Any of these work for my purposes, I think the last on is a little too strict of a check, but only because of how loose `namedtuple` comparisons are.

issue_title: validate field should prior to make_object
issue_body:
Version: marshmallow==2.0.0b4

Example:
```
class UserSchema(Schema):
    github_account_name = Str()

    @validates('github_account_name')
    def validate_github_account_name(self, value):
          ........

    def make_object(self, data):
          return User(**data)
```
This example lead to a error in line 771(value = data[field_name]) in schema.py, alleging 

```
TypeError: 'User' object has no attribute '__getitem__'
```

The problem is that the "value" in line 771 is actually the User object which returned by make_object, the 'field_name ' is exactly 'github_account_name' that defined needs to be validated.

This problem may be solved by validate fields prior to make_object.

issue_title: gaussian process regression to estimate PV performance
issue_body:
Historical system and weather data can be used to develop PV models.  In the past, least-squares regression has been used to model systems based on past data.  However, the Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) algorithm can provide a more accurate estimate of PV performance. 
![graph_model_power](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/5728325/22664287/32889cb6-ec6d-11e6-9e76-5e929d00df66.png)
The algorithm can estimate power, current, and voltage with a smaller % error than the least-squares approach: 
![graph_mape_power](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/5728325/22664362/99b1adba-ec6d-11e6-9178-f274e44024cb.png)
The above figure shows the percent error for estimated power based on three different training data sets: 1) complete set, 2) binned, and 3) random.  The GPR reached 5% while the least-squares regression did not drop below 8%.

There are other Python packages that provide GPR algorithms.  However, it may be difficult for people that do not have a machine learning background to implement.  I propose to provide a GPR algorithm that is customized for PV applications.

**Do you think that it would be valuable to add GPR capabilities to the existing PVLIB tool set?**

Here is sample code for the GPR function:

def kernel(sigma,alpha,l,x1,x2):
	
        '''
	Rational Quadratic Kernel
	:param: sigma (sigma^2 determines average difference of function from mean)
	:param: alpha (determines relative weighting of variations)
	:param: l (length scale)
	:param: x1 - training set input
	:param: x2 - teting set input
	
	:return: kernel
	'''
	s = distance.cdist(x1,x2,'euclidean')
	k = (sigma**2) * (1+((s)**2/(2*alpha*l**2)))**-alpha
	return k
		
def gp(x1,y1,x2,sigf,alpha,l,sign=0.1):
    
    K = kernel(sigf,alpha,l,x1,x1)				
    Kss = kernel(sigf,alpha,l,x2,x2)
    Ks = kernel(sigf,alpha,l,x1,x2)
    Ks = np.transpose(Ks)	
    K = K + np.identity(len(K)) * noise								# Add noise
    L = np.linalg.cholesky(K)  
    beta = np.linalg.solve(L.transpose(), np.linalg.solve(L,y1))
    Mu = np.array([np.dot(Ks,beta)]).T
    
    d = np.dot(Ks,inv(K))
    m = np.dot(d,np.transpose(Ks))
    s = Kss - m
    di = np.diag(s)
    Sigma = np.transpose(np.array([np.sqrt(di)]))
  
    return Mu,Sigma

issue_title: POST requests not working
issue_body:
I am integrating a `django-rest-framework` backend and an `Ember` frontend (with `EmberData`). These are my versions:

  - Django 1.9
  - djangorestframework 3.3.2
  - django-rest-framework-json-api (latest version from github)
  - ember-cli 1.13.13
  - ember 2.2.0
  - ember-data 2.2.1

GET requests are working fine, but POSTs are not. This is the kind of request coming from EmberData:

```
{
  "data": {
    "attributes": {
      "name": "The project name",
      "description": "The project description",
      "price": 123
    },
    "relationships": {
      "onwer": {
        "data": null
      }
    },
    "type": "projects"
  }
}
```

But django is crashing, and reporting:

```
Page not found ... The current URL, api/projects, didn't match any of these
```

My URLs are created with a router:

```
from rest_framework.routers import DefaultRouter

from .viewsets.projects import ProjectViewSet


router = DefaultRouter()
...
router.register(r'projects', ProjectViewSet)
...
```

and `ViewSet`:

```
from rest_framework import viewsets
from rest_framework import filters

from vwrks.models import Project

from ..serializers import ProjectSerializer

class ProjectViewSet(viewsets.ModelViewSet):
    """
    A viewset for viewing and editing Project instances.
    """
    serializer_class = ProjectSerializer
    queryset = Project.objects.all()
    filter_backends = (filters.DjangoFilterBackend, filters.SearchFilter)
    filter_fields = ('seller', 'artist', 'subcategory')
    search_fields = ('name', 'description')
```

According to the documentation (http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/viewsets/#modelviewset), `ViewSet`s provide support for `.create()`, so this POST operation should be supported.

I have also tweaked the trailing slashes for Django, in order to accept `POST`s at `/projects` as `EmberData` is doing (besides the default `/projects/` that Django is expecting)

```
# EmberData does POST to /projects (not /projects/), so we need this:
APPEND_SLASH = False
```

The problem that I see is that `Django`'s router does not generate a `project-create` URL (only `project-list` and `project-detail` are listed):

```
^api/ ^projects/$ [name='project-list']
^api/ ^projects\.(?P<format>[a-z0-9]+)/?$ [name='project-list']
^api/ ^projects/(?P<pk>[^/.]+)/$ [name='project-detail']
^api/ ^projects/(?P<pk>[^/.]+)\.(?P<format>[a-z0-9]+)/?$ [name='project-detail']
```

issue_title: Custom arguments problems are not displayed correctly anymore.
issue_body:
Senza definition to reproduce:

```yaml
SenzaInfo:
  StackName: senza-args-test
  Parameters:
  - Arg1:
      Description: noop arg
  - Arg2:
      Description: noop arg
  - Arg3:
      Description: noop arg
  - Arg4:
      Description: noop arg
```

Now call `senza print` which requires properly filled in arguments of our definition:

```bash
$ senza print senza-parameter-test.yaml
Usage: senza print [OPTIONS] DEFINITION VERSION [PARAMETER]...

Error: Missing argument "version".
```

First regression comes in the "Usage" line: Senza doesn't print out the defined arguments anymore but only a generic "[PARAMETER]" block which isn't helpful. Expected message:

```bash
Usage: senza print [OPTIONS] DEFINITION VERSION arg1 arg2 arg3 arg4

Error: Missing argument "version".
```

If specifying more arguments, the error message becomes random:

```bash
$ senza print senza-parameter-test.yaml 0
Usage: senza print [OPTIONS] DEFINITION VERSION [PARAMETER]...

Error: Missing parameter "Arg3"

$ senza print senza-parameter-test.yaml 0
Usage: senza print [OPTIONS] DEFINITION VERSION [PARAMETER]...

Error: Missing parameter "Arg1"

$ senza print senza-parameter-test.yaml 0
Usage: senza print [OPTIONS] DEFINITION VERSION [PARAMETER]...

Error: Missing parameter "Arg4"

$ senza print senza-parameter-test.yaml 0
Usage: senza print [OPTIONS] DEFINITION VERSION [PARAMETER]...

Error: Missing parameter "Arg2"
```

Also when specifying more parameters, it doesn't get better:

```bash
$ senza print senza-parameter-test.yaml 0 1 2
Usage: senza print [OPTIONS] DEFINITION VERSION [PARAMETER]...

Error: Missing parameter "Arg4"

$ senza print senza-parameter-test.yaml 0 1 2
Usage: senza print [OPTIONS] DEFINITION VERSION [PARAMETER]...

Error: Missing parameter "Arg3"
```

Previous behaviour was like that:

```bash
$ senza print senza-parameter-test.yaml
Usage: senza print [OPTIONS] DEFINITION VERSION arg1 arg2 arg3 arg4

Error: Missing argument "version".

$ senza print senza-parameter-test.yaml 0
Usage: senza print [OPTIONS] DEFINITION VERSION arg1 arg2 arg3 arg4

Error: Missing argument "arg1".

$ senza print senza-parameter-test.yaml 0 1
Usage: senza print [OPTIONS] DEFINITION VERSION arg1 arg2 arg3 arg4

Error: Missing argument "arg2".

$ senza print senza-parameter-test.yaml 0 1 2
Usage: senza print [OPTIONS] DEFINITION VERSION arg1 arg2 arg3 arg4

Error: Missing argument "arg3".

$ senza print senza-parameter-test.yaml 0 1 2 3
Usage: senza print [OPTIONS] DEFINITION VERSION arg1 arg2 arg3 arg4

Error: Missing argument "arg4".

$ senza print senza-parameter-test.yaml 0 1 2 3 4
< all args passed, print! >
```

This regression is very unpleasant because depending of the amount of arguments of a senza definition, you cannot find out what to pass to the command and in which order.

issue_title: Optimization with recursive functions yield incorrect result
issue_body:
Thanks @NikolajBjorner Your recent fixes to recursive functions allowed me to make progress. Alas, I'm still seeing bizarre behavior.

This script:

```
(declare-datatypes  () ((Item (mk-item (size Int) (weight Int)))))
(declare-fun items  () (Array Int Item))
(declare-fun picked () (Array Int Bool))

(assert (= (select items 1) (mk-item 4 2)))
(assert (= (select items 2) (mk-item 4 2)))
(assert (= (select items 3) (mk-item 1 4)))
(assert (= (select items 4) (mk-item 5 5)))
(assert (= (select items 5) (mk-item 3 2)))
(assert (= (select items 6) (mk-item 1 9)))

(define-fun-rec computeSize ((lb Int) (ub Int)) Int
   (ite (> lb ub)
        0
        (+ (ite (select picked lb) (size (select items lb)) 0)
           (computeSize (+ lb 1) ub))))

(define-fun-rec computeWeight ((lb Int) (ub Int)) Int
   (ite (> lb ub)
        0
        (+ (ite (select picked lb) (weight (select items lb)) 0)
           (computeWeight (+ lb 1) ub))))

(define-fun totalSize   () Int (computeSize 1 6))
(define-fun totalWeight () Int (computeWeight 1 6))
(assert (< totalSize 10))
(maximize totalWeight)

(check-sat)
(get-value (totalSize))
(get-value (totalWeight))
(eval (select picked 1))
(eval (select picked 2))
(eval (select picked 3))
(eval (select picked 4))
(eval (select picked 5))
(eval (select picked 6))
```

produces:

```
sat
((totalSize 7))
((totalWeight 4))
false
true
false
false
true
false
```

Unfortunately, that is the incorrect answer. There is a solution such that the `totalWeight` is actually `18`, by picking items 3, 4, and 6.

When I code the whole thing without recursive functions, I get the correct answer indeed. In fact, if you remove:

```
(maximize totalWeight)
```

And replace it with:

```
(assert (>= totalWeight 18))
```

that also works as well, producing:

```
sat
((totalSize 7))
((totalWeight 18))
false
false
true
true
false
true
```

So, I'm not sure what to blame here; but seems to be an interaction between optimization and recursively defined functions.

issue_title: new untitled notebooks get a kernel but do not record kernelspec
issue_body: The empty untitled notebook you get when you launch nteract gets hooked up to a default kernel, but that information doesn't get recorded in notebook.metadata.kernelspec.

issue_title: Add buttons for creating a new cell in between existing cells in UI
issue_body:
This is a modest proposal that I'll aim to put a mock up for later.

Between each cell should be a little area where we can click to create new cells. This could also be the drop target, though I think I backed away from that idea in #135.

When you hover over the area between cells, it shows icons for creating a text or code area.

issue_title: Improve reading notebooks from url that need accept json header
issue_body:
Our notebooks are being stored in an [nbgallery](https://github.com/nbgallery/nbgallery) instance.  A generic `requests.get(notebook_url)` returns html.  Papermill's `execute.execute_notebook(notebook_url)` / `iowr.HttpHandler.read` do not offer a way to set a `{'Accept' : 'application/json'}` header in the request.

When we make a request on our own using accept json headers, we can write that notebook to file (`tempfile` or otherwise) and use Papermill as is.  It would be convenient to also be able to pass in a file-like object (`io.StringIO(resp.text)`), json (`resp.text`), and/or dictionary (`resp.json()`) of the downloaded notebook file rather than needing to write to file.

Thanks.

issue_title: posisbility start paremetrized notebook server instead of only headless execution / write parametrized notebook to *.ipynb
issue_body:
I love the run_notebook option and this is probably the main use-case, but sometimes i'd like to be able (or better have a service to which I don't have direct access to like binder) to start an interactive notebook server but with the parameters predetermined

a la
```
papermill save --input input.ipynb --parameter a='hello world' --output output.ipynb
jupyter notebook.ipynb
```

or better

```
papermill serve input.ipynb --parameter a='hello world' 
```

would others see this as useful as well?

issue_title: Move content from zh-cn to zh-Hans
issue_body:
It looks like django has moved its language code from zh-cn to zh-Hans.

How can content using wagtail-modeltranslation be moved from one language code to the other ?

issue_title: PointerWrapper is missing
issue_body:
At the bottom of https://docs.angr.io/docs/structured_data.html, `b.factory.callable.PointerWrapper` is mentioned if I try to pass a pointer to the Callable, but in angr 6.7.1.31, it seems that it is missing.
Now I try to make a constructor Callable, and use it to init an object. The constructor is in the form of `bool MyClass(void *this)`. So how can I allocate an BVV, pass its address to it and let it init my object? Thanks.

issue_title: DPKY2.0 Race condition in Vehicle.battery
issue_body:
The vehicle_state.py example fails with 
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "D:\github\dronekit-python\examples\vehicle_state\vehicle_state.py", line 23, in <module>
    print " Battery: %s" % vehicle.battery
  File "build\bdist.win-amd64\egg\droneapi\module\api.py", line 182, in battery
  File "build\bdist.win-amd64\egg\droneapi\lib\__init__.py", line 181, in __init__
TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for /: 'NoneType' and 'float'
```
I think this is is due to a race condition. We are getting the Battery object before it is initialised with a real value. The result is a divide by None error because the default value is set to None in the interface. You can reproduce this using code like below ... and if you uncomment the timer before the print for a few seconds the error does not occur.
```
from droneapi import connect
from droneapi.lib import VehicleMode
from pymavlink import mavutil
import time

api = connect('127.0.0.1:14550')
vehicle = api.get_vehicles()[0]
#time.sleep(1)
print " Battery: %s" % vehicle.battery
time.sleep(3)
print 'exit'
```

issue_title: 平仓时，有时会出现连续两笔平仓单都能成交的情况
Lacking nanosecond pcap support
issue_body:
Hi,

We have a PCAP file with _nanosecond precision_ timestamps.
When we open it using `pypcap`, it gives us timestamps with _microsecond precision_.

There seems to be a fork of this project which does seem to have support for the different pcap timestamp precisions (_according to the source code_):
https://github.com/fasguard/fasguard-pcap/blob/master/fasguard_pcap/__init__.pyx

Would it be possible to include the timestamp precision support in this project?

With best regards,
Tom.

I would like to determine the actual gain and exposure times for the frames of the ZR300's fisheye camera. I am using librealsense `master` branch from this repository.

In order to investigate I modified the `cpp-config-ui` example a little bit to add output for every fisheye frame: exposure time in metadata of the frame, as well as exposure time and gain from `dev->get_options(...)`. issue_title: Serialized VaspInputSets don't contain user_incar_settings added after instantiation
issue_body:
It is sometimes the case that I would like to use one customized input set as a base and change the parameters (e.g. ISIF) programmatically later down the line.
The most straightforward way I can see to do this is to update the `user_incar_settings` dict after instantiating the input set. 
In code, what I want to do is:

``` python
def relax_743(structure, my_input_set):
    vis1 = copy(my_input_set)
    vis1.user_incar_settings.update({'ISIF': 7})
    fw1 = OptimizeFW(structure, vasp_input_set=vis1)

    vis2 = copy(my_input_set)
    vis2.user_incar_settings.update({'ISIF': 4})
    fw2 = OptimizeFW(structure, vasp_input_set=vis2, parents=fw1)

    vis3 = copy(my_input_set)
    vis3.user_incar_settings.update({'ISIF': 3})
    fw3 = OptimizeFW(structure, vasp_input_set=vis3, parents=fw2)
    
    fws = [fw1, fw2, fw3]
    return Workflow(fws)
```

When doing this, serializing the input sets doesn't work:

```python
from pymatgen import Structure
from pymatgen.io.vasp.sets import MPRelaxSet

### Setting during initialization

vis = MPRelaxSet(Structure.from_file('POSCAR'), user_incar_settings={'ISIF': 7})

print(vis.as_dict()['user_incar_settings'])
# prints:
# {'ISIF': 7}


### Setting after initialization

vis = MPRelaxSet(Structure.from_file('POSCAR'))

print(vis.user_incar_settings)
# prints (as expected, we didn't set any user incar settings)
# {}

vis.user_incar_settings.update({'ISIF': 7})
print(vis.user_incar_settings)
# prints (as expected, we just set this)
# {'ISIF': 7}

print(vis.as_dict()['user_incar_settings'])
# KeyError! The INCAR settings are not serialized
```

@shyuep is this the intended behavior of the VaspInputSets?

If it is the intended behavior, is there a better way to do this? In atomate, we pass everything by instance, but maybe we should instantiate inside the `Firework.__init__` method (for the subclass of Firework) and instead pass the *class* `MPRelaxSet` rather than an instance of `MPRelaxSet` as arguments to the Fireworks?

issue_title: gitChangeset leaves netpyne in wrong cwd if not under git
issue_body:
If the version of NetPyNE being used is locally installed (e.g. /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/netpyne-0.7.7-py2.7.egg/) and that dir is not under git, the gitChangeset() methods fails (which is correct behaviour).

But [this change](https://github.com/Neurosim-lab/netpyne/compare/neuroml_export...development#diff-a52fad7191c089fb6b67f5f3c6d75043L1769) leaves the current working dir in the install folder when it fails and means that methods to save the data try to write relative to that dir (and probably don't have permission).

Any reason to change from the prev func, of reverting back to currentPath even if it fails?

It seems httplib2 is calculating `conn_key` by  [this line](https://github.com/httplib2/httplib2/blob/master/python2/httplib2/__init__.py#L1516), it doesn't care about the greenlet, however greenlet doesn't like share a connection with each other. `conn_key = ":".join([threading.current_thread().name, scheme, authority])`

issue_title: Interop with Numba?
issue_body:
This is very far left field, but it'd be interesting if there were a way for `pybind11` and [`numba`](https://numba.pydata.org/) to interoperate.

One scenario might be if a `pybind` signature overload could be detected during `@numba.jit`, and if `numba` could detect `pybind` types, get the underlying C[++] data, and strip out marshaling normally done by `pybind`, and reduce the overhead when calling C++ code from Python in loops  (related: #1227).

issue_title: Fetch results from remote host (sos fetch -r HOST)
issue_body:
With the updated remote execution model, we can execute a workflow on remote host. Everything happens on remote hosts and it is users responsibility to copy input files over and retrieve results if needed.

Although it is not too difficult for users to synchronize files with command `rsync`, I am wondering if we can simplify this process with a subcommand. For example,

```
sos fetch result.txt -r HOST
```
would fetch `result.txt` from `HOST`, using the `path_map` that has been defined in `hosts.yml`.

If pushing files to remote host is also necessary, perhaps we can implement

```
sos push input_file -r HOST
sos pull output_file -r HOST
```

In 5300b154a, RandomScaleXY was added with constructor signature:

```python

```

https://github.com/fastai/fastai/commit/5300b154af0641580f67708eeb26d981fdf465d1#diff-48685f6197acf069ae438de1ac77a613R320

Later in the same file, there is a definition for image_gen, where RandomScaleXY is used:

```python

issue_title: Splitting up torchtext/data.py into files for each class
issue_body:
As it currently stands, [torchtext/data.py](https://github.com/pytorch/text/blob/master/torchtext/data.py) has a _lot_ of functionality in one file. I think it'd be nice to split the file into pieces separated by individual functionality, e.g. a file for `Fields`, a file for `Datasets` (and their subclasses, of course), a file for `Iterators`, etc. In particular, I think the structure of [torch.nn](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/tree/master/torch/nn) would work well here.

I think doing this would be a lot clearer in terms of organization of the codebase, but the biggest issue I have with it is that it changes the syntax for the import from `from torchtext import data` to `import torchtext.data as data` (much as one would do `import torch.nn as nn`), rendering the code backwards-incompatible. But i'm not sure how much weight is placed on this, considering the repo isn't on pypi yet and there's a big WIP on the readme...what do you all think?

issue_title: Actor creation tasks with completely infeasible resource requirements do not give warning messages.
issue_body:
First run the following code.

```python
import ray

ray.init(num_cpus=1)

@ray.remote(num_cpus=1, num_gpus=3, resources={"Custom": 2})
class Foo(object):
    pass

f = Foo.remote()
```

The actor cannot be created because the relevant resources are not available. However, because we only do spillback on the "dispatch" queue and not on the "waiting" queue, we never spillback the actor creation task, and so the warning is never triggered.

issue_title: Swap array ordering on components
issue_body:
One thing we've worried about a lot in working on CliMT is array ordering, especially C versus F ordering. After looking at the code, what we're currently doing is a little strange, and could be okay, but we should think about it and decide if we want to change it. Our two main considerations are that we want to minimize array copying, and we want things to work.

TL;DR: We should probably swap the dimension ordering on components.

How things currently work:

- We decided on a consistent dimension ordering among components, so that swapping dimensions between components isn't necessary. Currently that's `[horizontal, vertical]` or `[x, y, z]`, in C-ordering.
- The column-wise Fortran codes are generally written to take in arrays in `[horizontal, vertical]` (or `[column, vertical]`) order in F-ordering. This corresponds to `[vertical, horizontal]` in C-ordering. *If* we had any non-one number of columns passed into the Fortran codes, this would cause an error. However, the way we've written the wrappers, they pass in one column at a time to the Fortran code, so the ordering of the Fortran dimensions is actually irrelevant.

Some changes we might want to make:
- The code might run faster if we passed in multiple columns to the Fortran code at once, but I'm not sure. This would require testing. It would be the difference between applying a function F to each column of the model sequentially, and taking all columns and applying each step of the function F to all those columns at once. If we wanted to do this, we would need to switch CliMT's array ordering to be `[vertical, horizontal]`.
- I suppose we should probably just do whatever the dynamical core does? Which I think involves switching CliMT's array ordering to be `[vertical, horizontal]`. If we don't, we definitely need to copy when calling the dynamical core. If we switch the ordering for this reason, we *must* pass multiple columns at once to the Fortran codes, or we'd again have array copying.
- After switching, CliMT's Python and Fortran codes would use the same memory ordering arrays, [vertical, horizontal] in the C-ordered Python code, and [horizontal, vertical] in the F-ordered Fortran code.

issue_title: Design philosophy of ipywidgets wrt same-kernel collaboration
issue_body:
Currently, same-kernel collaboration is not working smoothly with ipywidgets.
I start a notebook session and enter the following cell:

```python
import ipywidgets
ipywidgets.IntSlider()
```
When I modify the cell, embed the widget state, and save the notebook, the widget will show up when I load the notebook URL in a different browser. 
In the new notebook session, the widget will modify the kernel value. However, the widget in the two sessions are no longer in auto-sync with the kernel: in each session, I have to press F5 to see any updates in the kernel value that were effectuated by the other session.

I am not sure if this is a bug or a design decision.  [This discussion](https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/issues/65#issue-90668032) seems on-topic, but mostly talks about security and locking, which I don't care about. In my case, in each notebook session, the code cells will be hidden from the user and only the widgets are being modified.

I am thinking of rolling my own interactive widget implementation (where the kernel simply launches a websocketserver, bypassing Jupyter completely). But if it is simply an ipywidgets bug, I won't try to re-invent the wheel.

issue_title: Better support for multiple tracers/services
issue_body:
In some cases it is useful to simulate the existence of different services from within the same process, e.g. when making queries to services that are not instrumented (e.g. databases). In this case we'd want the `service` on the span to be the remote service.

However, the only way to do this is to create multiple tracers. I came across this proposal https://github.com/opentracing/specification/issues/77 for adding a `service` tag, but it has not been fleshed out.

The go client makes it pretty straightforward to create multiple tracers, by having methods on the `config` object to create a `Sampler` and a `Reporter`. However the python client does all the heavy lifting inside the `initialize_tracer` method, which can only be called once.

Should we break out factory methods here like we do in the go client? (see https://github.com/jaegertracing/jaeger-client-go/blob/master/config/config.go#L269).

issue_title: Unable to use self-signed certs - CN incorrect, and/or missing subject alternative name field
issue_body:
When enabling SSL for the services (required as the Kinesis producer will only support HTTPS), I would like to add the generated self-signed cert to my java truststore so that I can interact with the services. In some instances, I can disable SSL verification, but in others I cannot (for example, when using the Jest ES client library). 

I have tried adding the generated certs to my truststore, however, there is no Subject Alternative Name field, and the CN on the cert doesn't match the host (localhost in this instance), so I'm unable to make use of the cert. If I add a `localstack` entry to my hosts file, it works.

The error essentially looks like this:

```
Caused by: javax.net.ssl.SSLPeerUnverifiedException: Certificate for <localhost> doesn't match any of the subject alternative names: []
```
Please consider setting the certificate CN to the configured hostname (defaulting to localhost), and or add the subject alternative name field to the cert, which would include the various DNS entries to enable use of the cert.

issue_title: Download improvement ideas
issue_body:
I have been thinking for a while about possibly replacing `homura` with something custom-made based on `requests`. Here's my reasoning:
* A major drawback of homura is that we have no direct control over downloading, a significant part of sentinelsat.
* Homura does not check for HTTP error statuses, which can cause it to append junk to the downloaded files. This is a frequent situation on SciHub.
* This will open up the opportunity to add parallel download support. My idea here would be to split the download for each file into *n* parts, one for each download thread, each part being saved as "<*product*>.zip.part<*i*>", which will be merged together once the download is complete and checked. This naming scheme will make it easier to identify incomplete downloads in the download directory.
* [tqdm](https://github.com/tqdm/tqdm) can be used to show download progress, speed and ETA. An additional progressbar can also be added to display overall download progress for multiple files. tqdm is already used during checksum calculation in sentinelsat.
* Gets rid of the resuming of > 2 GB files [bug](https://github.com/ibamacsr/sentinelsat/blob/master/sentinelsat/sentinel.py#L388) in `pycurl`.
* If logging is implemented, then more fine-grained information and debug output can be logged.
* No need to worry about `certifi` being installed or not.
* By using `requests` the downloading functionality becomes mockable with `vcrpy` as well. We will be also able to simulate edge cases, like download errors, better for testing.

What are your thoughts on these proposed changes?

issue_title: Strange training results?
issue_body:
I have created a very simple gym environment and some test code on top of keras-rl. The simple gym environment is representing a ventilation fan with 4 speeds. The reward is extremely simple based on fan speed, the higher speed equals less reward. Optimal solution is to keep the fan speed at 0.

The problem is that both training and testing of the model experiences some very "bad" results when it comes to achieving a reasonably high reward as shown in this chart:

![rlperformance](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3481146/39576873-ed88da28-4edf-11e8-8a3e-d07d47a62c7c.JPG)

The chart is based on running 1 episode (with 60 steps) of training, then 1 episode (60 steps) of test. Then it's repeated in a loop. 

The chart shows the returned reward for returned by "fit" (training) and "test" for each iteration in the loop. The chart shows that there are severe drops in performance of the agent and it does not seem to improve over time either. It looks like it gets trained reasonably well over the first 20 iterations, but after that it behaves a bit "randomly" both when it comes to training and testing. I'm using eps=0.01, so I wouldn't think that it should behave this bad.

My goal would be to get it to perform a lot more consistent. I expect some dips due to exploration, but the chart results looks like it's not doing well.

What can be the reasons for the behavior seen?

The complete code can be found here:

https://github.com/olavt/gym_co2_ventilation (example file used is test_keras_rl_continious.py)
